coredata.dc:title: Lattice-matched carbon dots induced the oriented self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Piao-Yang
Liu, Yong-Lin
Lin, Zuan-Tao
Huang, Jian-Fei
Chen, Wen-Zhao
Liang, Jun-Hua
Zhou, Wuyi
Jiang, Gang-Biao
coredata.dc:description: Orientation-induced crystallization is a widely applied method of forming crystal with highly oriented structure. Here, we reported a promising matrix for orientation-induced crystallization–carbon dots (CDs), who exhibited high lattice-matching with Cu crystal. First, CDs induce self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles through organics-inorganics interface interaction, forming self-assembled mesocrystal and single crystal with large size. Second, the CDs selectively deposit on Cu {111} plane via lattice-matching, endowing Cu self-assemblies with tunable morphology including sphere, truncated octahedron, octahedron, concave octahedron and hexpods. Third, CDs also protect Cu self-assemblies from oxidation and act as efficient interface for optical conversion and electron transfer. So using as surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate for the first time, the CDs-coating hexpods Cu mesocrystal require no post processing and exhibit high stability and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity which ordinary Cu nanostructure cannot achieve.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of various Cu mesocrystal, monocrystal and polycrystal
Controlled experiments about components affecting the formation of self-assembly in CDs matrix
SERS activity evaluation on the Cu hexpods mesocrystal substrate
CDs-induced formation of self-assembled concave octahedral mesocrystal
CDs-induced formation of self-assembled micro-octahedral Cu single crystal
Carbon dots-induced anisotropic evolution of self-assemblies
CDs-induced anisotropic evolution mechanism of self-assemblies
Self-assembled hexpods Cu mesocrystals for SERS substrate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully thank for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41671302), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2014B090901037, 2015A010105022, 2016A010103026); Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City (201604020134, 201607010183).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–b) FESEM image of Cu concave octahedral mesocrystal; (c) FESEM image of semi-finished concave octahedral mesocrystal, (top) building block of self-assembled concave octahedral mesocrystal; (d) FETEM image, (upper right) SEAD, and (bottom right) larger vision of selected area of Cu concave octahedral mesocrystal; (e) FETEM image of carbon dots (CDs), (top) photos of flourescent CDs excited by 365 nm; (f–g) FETEM images of selected larger vision of Cu concave octahedral mesocrystal; (h) EDS spectrum of selected area in Fig. 1a; (i) XRD pattern of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal powder. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Time-dependent FESEM images of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystals after (a) 20 min; (b) 8 h; (c) 16 h; (d–i) FESEM images of Cu particles with different growth state obtained after 16 h (d) self-assembled blocks; (e) directional aggregation of self-assembled blocks; (f) self-assembly; (g–i) anisotropic evolution of self-assembly from octahedron to concave octahedron.

(a) SEM image of octahedral Cu single crystal; (b) semi-finished octahedral Cu single crystal; (c) FETEM image of nanosheet occurred in Fig. 3d; (d–f) SAED pattern (d), FETEM image (e) and larger vision (f) of octahedral Cu monocrystal; (g) XRD pattern of octahedral Cu single crystal; (h–i) FETEM images of CDs on the surface and gap between adjacent building blocks of self-assembled Cu single crystal. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–f) FESEM images of Cu self-assemblies with tunable morphology were obtained after 16 h at (a) 0.5 (5); (b) 1 (5); (c) 2 (5); (d) 1 (25); (e–f) 2 (25). (g–i) FESEM images of Cu particles obtained after 16 h when the volume ratio of OH− (10 M) to Cu2+ (0.1 M) was 25 and CDs solution was replaced by raw organics including (g) chitosan-acetic acid and TEPA; (h) chitosan-acetic acid solution; (i) TEPA solution. Detailed information was shown in controlled experiment 2.

(a) Raman spectra of 10−4 M (red), 10−3 M (black), 10−6 M (blue) and 10−8 M (violet) 4-ATP on Cu hexpods SERS substrate, and 10−3 M 4-ATP (green) on the glass slide; (b) Raman spectra of 10−6 M 4-ATP on selected six; The excitation intensity, accumulation time and frequency were 0.17 mW, 10 s and 1 time separately. (c) XRD pattern of obtained hexpods Cu mesocrystal kept in air at room temperature after 40 days; (d) TGA-DTG curve of hexpods Cu mesocrystal in air. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of anisotropic growth mechanism when the growth condition of Cu mesocrystal changed from 0.5 (5) to 2 (5), and from 1 (25) to 4 (25).
Indexing of close lattice spacing (d) between Cu and CDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: dcu refer to JCPDS card of face-centered cubic copper (no.04-0836).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: dCDs refer to the value of CDs from the literature [15].

head.ce:title: Lattice-matched carbon dots induced the oriented self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Energy, South China Agriculture University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of Houston, TX, 77204, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Orientation-induced crystallization is a widely applied method of forming crystal with highly oriented structure. Here, we reported a promising matrix for orientation-induced crystallization–carbon dots (CDs), who exhibited high lattice-matching with Cu crystal. First, CDs induce self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles through organics-inorganics interface interaction, forming self-assembled mesocrystal and single crystal with large size. Second, the CDs selectively deposit on Cu {111} plane via lattice-matching, endowing Cu self-assemblies with tunable morphology including sphere, truncated octahedron, octahedron, concave octahedron and hexpods. Third, CDs also protect Cu self-assemblies from oxidation and act as efficient interface for optical conversion and electron transfer. So using as surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate for the first time, the CDs-coating hexpods Cu mesocrystal require no post processing and exhibit high stability and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity which ordinary Cu nanostructure cannot achieve.

CDs induce self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles through organics-inorganics interface interaction, forming self-assembled mesocrystal and single crystal with large size. CDs selectively deposit on Cu {111} plane via lattice-matching and epitaxial growth, endowing Cu self-assemblies with tunable morphology including sphere, truncated octahedron, octahedron, concave octahedron and hexpods.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the special structure of “quantum dots” core and remaining organic shell [1,2], carbon dots (CDs) are effective for optical conversion and electron transfer, and are widely applied in fluorescent probe, biosensor, catalysis, energy storage and conversion [3]. This special structure also enables CDs to induce crystallization of inorganics as organic matrix. Gao and Shen found that the inorganic atoms would be induced nucleation on the surface of CDs and formed CDs-inorganic hybrid materials [4]. CDs also induce aggregation of nanoparticles due to interaction between organics in CDs [5]. CDs-inorganic hybrid materials are easily modified and have improved photoelectrical properties and catalytic activities, because CDs acted as effective interface for modification, stabilization, optical conversion and electron transfer [6]. However, can CDs induce oriented crystallization and aggregation? Thus we can obtain highly ordered structure with enhanced photoelectrical properties and catalytic activities, like high-quality single crystal for photoelectronics and mesocrystal with plentiful active sites for catalysis [7,8]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is still no research about it.

Lattice-matched substrate induce oriented crystallization is an importance method for construction of highly ordered structure [9,10]. Cu is the most early used metal, it was widely applied in catalysis, conductor paste, flexible electrode, photoelectric material, lubrication and antibiosis [11–14]. Cu crystal exhibits high lattice-matching with CDs [15]. So it is promising to obtain ordered Cu structure with enhanced photoelectrical properties and catalytic activities through CDs-induced oriented crystallization and aggregation.

As a kind of powerful and specific analytic technique, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is promising in the field of sensor at trace level [16–20]. Coinage metals (Au, Ag, and Cu) are widely used as active SERS substrates because their strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect even are excited by visible light [21–23]. However, Cu is cost-effective in contrast to noble metal (Au and Ag), but few studies about Cu based SERS substrates are reported because the results of previous studies showed their weak SERS effects and poor thermal and chemical stability [24–26]. Oriented aggregation of Cu nanoparticles (self-assembly) would be an effective strategy to overcome these drawbacks for the following reasons: 1. Self-assemblies would have high SERS activity due to their abundant and ordered “hot-spots” structure such as gaps and tips formed by orderly close stacking [27–29]; 2. Self-assemblies have higher stability for aggregation-induced surface energy decrease than nanoparticles.

In this study, three-dimensional Cu self-assemblies were fabricated by using lattice-matched CDs as growth additive, and acted as a SERS substrate for the first time. We extensively investigated the growth process of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal to elucidate how CDs mediated the self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles, and achieved the control of self-assembled process. Time-dependent morphology evolution of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystals showed obviously anisotropic evolution of self-assemblies. This was leaded by CDs rather than organics because there was no anisotropic evolution when raw organics were used as a growth additive. It was noted that self-assembled process of mesocrystals could be divided into three steps. The first step was the formation of building blocks by the crystallization and fusion of primary nanoparticles. Polycrystal, mesocrystal and monocrystal were obtained by controlling the nucleation rate of primary nanoparticles. The second step was self-assembly of building blocks. CDs induced self-assembly of Cu nanoparticles through organics-inorganics interface interaction. The third step was anisotropic evolution of self-assembly. CDs selectively deposited on the Cu crystal plane of {111} via lattice matching, leading to anisotropic growth of self-assemblies. Finally, self-assembled Cu mesocrystal and single crystal with various morphology including sphere, truncated octahedron, octahedron, concave octahedron and hexpods were obtained in CDs matrix by controllable self-assembled process. Self-assembled hexpods Cu mesocrystal was the first attempt to use as a SERS substrate due to its possible dielectric sensitivity [30]. As one of the most popular probe molecules for SERS spectroscopy, p-aminothiophenol (4-ATP) molecule has strong affinity with metal nanoparticles and biomacromolecule, so it is potential as reporter in detection of biomacromolecule [31,32]. Using 4-ATP as a probe molecule on this SERS substrate, the detection limit is 10−8 M.

Chitosan (deacetylation > 95%) was purchased from Sanland Chemical Co. LTD. Tetraethylenepentamine (TEAP) (technical grade) was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Branched polyethylenimine (bPEI) (typical Mn 10000) (analytical grade) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Co. Citric acid (CA) (analytical grade), acetic acid (analytical grade), cupric chloride dihydrate (analytical grade), hydrazine hydrate (80%), and anhydrous ethanol (analytical grade) were purchased from TianJing DaMao Reagent Corporation.

0.04 g of chitosan was dissolved in 40 mL of 1% acetic acid solution, followed by the addition of 0.16 mL of TEPA. The mixture were stirred for 5 min to form homogeneous solution, then was transferred into 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature, and we obtained yellow CDs solution. Similarly, bPEI-CDs or CA-CDs were also separately synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of equal amount of chitosan acetic-acid solution with bPEI or CA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of various Cu mesocrystal, monocrystal and polycrystal
ce:section.ce:para: 40 mL of 0.1 M cupric chloride dihydrate was separately mixed with 20 mL, 40 mL and 80 mL of obtained CDs solution, making the final volume of mixtures to be 160 mL. 80 g of NaOH was then added into the mixtures. 6.4 mL of 35% hydrazine hydrate was added into the mixture dropwise under stirring at 70 °C. After 16 h, precipitates were centrifuged at 9800 rpm/min (HC-3518, ZONKIA, China) for 15 min, and then were washed with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol for six times. Octahedral, concave octahedral and hexpods Cu mesocrystals were obtained separately.

4 mL of 0.1 M cupric chloride dihydrate was separately mixed with 4 mL and 8 mL of obtained CDs solution, making the final volume of mixtures to be 80 mL. 40 g of NaOH was then added into the mixtures. 0.64 mL of 35% hydrazine hydrate was added into the mixture dropwise under stirring at 70 °C. The step was the same as that described above in synthesis of octahedral Cu mesocrystal. Finally, octahedral Cu monocrystal and superstructure containing sphere and truncated octahedron were obtained separately.

40 mL of 0.1 M cupric chloride dihydrate was mixed with 40 mL of obtained CDs solution, making final volume of mixtures to be 160 mL. 2 mL of 4 M NaOH was then added into the mixtures. The step was the same as that described above in synthesis of octahedral Cu mesocrystal. Cu polycrystal was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controlled experiments about components affecting the formation of self-assembly in CDs matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Controlled experiments were conducted to figure out it was CDs or residual organics, which coexisting in CDs matrix, that affected the formation of self-assembly. The raw organics solutions such as TEAP solution, chitosan acetic acid solution or mixture of them were used to synthesize the self-assembly instead of CDs separately.

Controlled experiments 1: Controlled experiments about concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal were carried out. 40 mL of 0.1 M cupric chloride dihydrate was separately mixed with 40 mL solution containing 0.04 g chitosan and 1% acetic acid, 40 mL solution containing 0.16 mL of TEAP and 40 mL of solution containing both of them, making the final volume of mixtures to be 160 mL, then 80 g of NaOH was added. The next step was same as the synthesis of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal.

Controlled experiments 2: The controlled experiments about octahedral Cu monocrystal were carried out. 4 mL of 0.1 M cupric chloride dihydrate was separately mixed with 4 mL solution containing 0.004 g chitosan and 1% acetic acid, 4 mL solution containing 0.016 mL of TEAP and 4 of mL solution containing both of them, making the final volume of mixtures to be 80 mL, then 80 g of NaOH was added. The next step was same as the synthesis of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal.

The morphology of obtained Cu particles was characterized by thermal field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, FEI Quanta-400F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30). The crystalline structure and component of obtained Cu particles were analyzed by a X-ray diffraction analyzer (XRD, Rigaku D/max-IIIA, Cu Kα (1.542 Å) radiation), EDS (Accessory of FESEM, Oxford) and SEAD (Accessory of TEM). The thermal behavior of obtained Cu particles was performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, Netzsch TG-209 F1). TGA was carried out at 10 °C/min in air or N2 atmosphere. The surface area and zeta potential of obtained Cu particles were measured by a surface area analyzer (BET, Micromeritics Gemini VII 2390) and a Malvern laser particle size analyzer (Mastersizer 3000). Raman spectra of obtained Cu particles and Cu-supported 4-ATP were performed by a Renishaw Invia system with a 633 nm excitation line, the excitation intensity, and accumulation time and frequency were 0.17 mW, and 10 s, and 1 time separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS activity evaluation on the Cu hexpods mesocrystal substrate
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of SERS substrate was followed the method previously reported by Kho et al. [33] Adhesion microscope slides with positive-charge surface treatment were rinsed with acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water for three times, then were blow-dried as standby application. Various concentrations of 4-ATP were mixed with 4 mg/mL Cu hexpods mesocrystal in ethanol, followed by sonication for 30 min. The mixtures were incubated for 12 h at 4 °C, and were dropped on the glass slides uniformly. Finally the glass slides containing Cu-supported 4-ATP were dried under vacuum at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-induced formation of self-assembled concave octahedral mesocrystal
ce:section.ce:para: The concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal was obtained at 1 (5) ― for convenience, the content outside the parentheses represented the volume ratio of obtained CDs to Cu2+ (0.1 M) and the inside represented the volume ratio of OH− (10 M) to Cu2+ (0.1 M). Fig. 1 a showed uniform size of this mesocrystal. Fig. 1b presented a kind of concave octahedral morphology, chosen amplifying images displayed that facet of Cu concave octahedron was rough, and this morphology was possibly formed by ordered aggregation of nanoparticles (Fig. 1b top). Obvious hollow structure could be found in semi-finished mesocrystal (Fig. 1c), this indicated the self-assembly in CDs matrix possibly through the simultaneous oriented attachment (OR) and Ostwald ripening (OA) mechanism [33]. The XRD pattern of obtained materials displayed that the three sharp diffraction peaks coincided with three main peaks of face-centered cubic copper (JCPDS card no.04-0836), separately representing the (111) plane, (200) plane, (220) plane and (311) plane of pure copper crystals (Fig. 1i). Additionally, energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) corroborated that the obtained product was pure copper crystal, because the copper element accounts for 87.78% At (Fig. 1h). From transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and field-emission TEM (FETEM), the mesocrystal can be confirmed for their obvious stacking architecture (Fig. 1d) which was also testified by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 3.1891 m2/g, and electron diffraction type between monocrystal and polycrystal (Fig. 1d upper right). The magnified image of selected area in Fig. 1d showed a layer of transparent film (Fig. 1d, bottom right). The films appeared on the surface of Cu mesocrystal when interaction time of electron beams and samples increased during FETEM analysis.

CDs are nanoparticles about 2 nm (Fig. 1e), they emitted blue fluorescence excited by 365 nm light (Fig. 1e top), the fluorescence spectrum of CDs was shown in Fig. S1. The CDs (111) plane (d = 0.302 nm) was observed on the surface of the mesocrystal obtained at CDs matrix (Fig. 1f). Fig. 1g showed the evident epitaxial growth of CDs (400) plane (d = 0.107 nm) from Cu (311) plane (d = 0.109 nm) because of low lattice mismatch (1.8%). The value of lattice mismatch was calculated using Equation (1):
(1)
m i s m a t c h =
|
d s
−
d f
|
d f
∗ 100 %
where d s was the lattice spacing of substrate, d f was the lattice spacing of epitaxial layer. Interestingly, as shown in Table 1 , there are various planes possessing close lattice spacing between CDs and Cu, for example, CDs (200) plane (d = 0.214 nm) and Cu (111) plane (d = 0.208 nm), CDs (211) plane (d = 0.174 nm) and Cu (200) plane (d = 0.180 nm), CDs (222) plane (d = 0.123 nm) from Cu (220) plane (d = 0.127 nm), and CDs (400) plane (d = 0.107 nm) and Cu (311) plane (d = 0.109 nm). Due to epitaxial growth of CDs via lattice-matching, they deposited on the Cu self-assemblies.

There are CDs and residual organics in CDs matrix, so which really worked for the self-assembly? Typically, the CDs consisted of a sp
2 or amorphous carbon framework and surface coated by functional group. In our experiments, CDs were synthesized by the reaction of chitosan with tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) through one-step hydrothermal method. The chitosan and TEPA separately acted as the framework and coating polymer chains in this approach. Control experiments were setup to ascertain the work of CDs (controlled experiments 1 in experiment part). Spherical and octahedral self-assembly emerged as well when a mixture of chitosan in 1% acetic-acid solution and TEPA was taken as a growth additive, but no concave architecture existed even after 24 h (Figs. S2a–S2b). When chitosan in 1% acetic-acid solution and TEPA was separately taken as a growth additive, the results were similar with that of mixture of them: only existence of spherical and octahedral but no concave architecture (Figs. S2c–S2d). So it was obvious that CDs resulted in the concave process of Cu self-assemblies. Different from templates with complex functionalization patterns, CDs is more accessible, various and environmental-friendly and it is significant to apply CDs in the self-assembly of inorganic materials. In practice, we obtained Cu high hierarchy mescocrystals under near-natural conditions in CDs matrix: 70 °C and ordinary pressure.

To elucidate the self-assembled process of self-assemblies, the time-dependent morphology evolution of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal was conducted by FESEM. All these results were obtained after copper (II) iron was reduced by hydrazine hydrate, along with that the color of the reaction solution changed from blue to pink and finally dark-red precipitate was formed. As shown in FESEM images, no half a concave octahedron existed in the whole 16 h growth and octahedron always appeared before the formation of concave octahedron. So we assumed the concave octahedral Cu mesocrystals growth process as self-assembled process taking place first then following a persistent concave process. After 20 min, building blocks with a size ranging from 200 nm to 600 nm were formed by coalescence of adjacent primary nanoparticles (Fig. 2 a), this indicated that these blocks possibly existed non-high-coincidence oriented relationship because of misorientation [34]. After 8 h, aggregations grew up and formed self-assembled structure of sphere or octahedron (Fig. 2b). When the volume ratio of obtained CDs to Cu2+ (0.1 M) was 1, most of shapes of self-assemblies were octahedron, but few spheres appeared simultaneously on the whole process, therefore, suggesting that these two architectures would hold similar energy state. As shown in Fig. 2c, these Cu mesocrystals finally possessed a kind of stable concave octahedral shape after 16 h.

The self-assembled process could divide into three steps in detail. Step 1, building blocks were formed by coalescence of adjacent primary nanoparticles (Fig. 2d). Step 2, self-assemblies was formed. Building blocks aggregated by oriented attachment (OA) and grew up through Ostwald ripening (OR) mechanism [35]. Firstly, a kind of hemispherical shell structure was found in primary self-assembled process, indicating that self-assembled process was an inside-out process (Fig. 2e). Next, octahedral self-assembly with 1 μm side length was possibly shaped by transformation from sphere, or directly self-assembly of building blocks through simultaneous OR and OA mechanism (Fig. 2e–g). Step 3, anisotropic growth of self-assemblies was mediated by CDs. We could find obvious concave process in this step. Meanwhile, the size of self-assemblies increased from 2 μm to 5 μm, following dent gradually appearing from central point of every facet in octahedron (Fig. 2h–i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-induced formation of self-assembled micro-octahedral Cu single crystal
ce:section.ce:para: It is reported that varying nucleation of primary nanoparticles would transform mesocrystal to single crystal and to polycrystal [36]. In this study, the transformation from mesocrystal to single crystal and polycrystal is achieved by the control of the volume ratio of OH− (10 M) to Cu2+ (0.1 M) which affected the nucleation rate of Cu primary nanoparticles (Fig. 3 ). Self-assembled Cu single crystal was obtained at 1 (25) while Cu mesocrystal was obtained at 1 (5). The XRD pattern of obtained Cu single crystal was presented in Fig. 3g, only subtle oxidation peak could be found. Fig. 3a showed that the shape of obtained material was octahedron with smoother surface in comparison with Fig. 1b. Many nanosheets as building blocks could be found in the FESEM and FETEM image (Fig. 3c top), the enlarged view of selected area in sheet showed that this sheet was formed by ordered aggregation of many nanoparticles with size of 3 nm (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3b exhibited semi-finished product of single crystal. Similar with the semi-finished mesocrystal, the hollow structure also could be found in it, indicating a similar self-assembled process with mesocrystal, but the building block of self-assembled single crystal is nanosheet while the building block of mesocrystal is nanoparticles (Figs, 3c, 1c). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns revealed that this self-assembled Cu octahedron was single crystal for ordered diffraction spots (Fig. 3d). The larger version of selected area of Fig. 3e was shown in Fig. 3f, we could observe the (111) lattice spacing (0.208 nm), the (-111) lattice spacing (0.208 nm) and the (200) lattice spacing (0.181 nm). Meanwhile, we also found that the included angle between (111) and (-111) was 70°, between (111) and (200) was 55°, and between (-111) and (200) was 55°. These results coincided with SEAD. Compared with primary nanoparticles of mesocrystal shown in Fig. 2d, the primary nanoparticles of single crystal had smaller size because of lower nucleation rate of Cu grain at higher relative concentration of NaOH. Meanwhile, smaller primary nanoparticles also faced lower resistance for grain-rotation-induced coalescence [36,37], benefiting from the formation of single crystal.

Polycrystal was obtained at 1 (0.02) (Fig. S3), because primary nanoparticles obtained in so fast nucleation rate could not be effectively stabilized by CDs. CDs (220) plane could be found on the surface of self-assembled Cu single crystal (Fig. 3h). In Fig. 3i, a large CDs (220) plane filled with the gap between adjacent building blocks, it hinted that CDs not only deposited on Cu building blocks via lattice matching-induced epitaxial growth, but also induced oriented crystallization and aggregation of Cu building blocks. The lattice spacing of CDs (220) plane (d = 0.214 nm) is close with exposed (111) plane of Cu (d = 0.208 nm) (Table 1), the lattice mismatch between them is only 2.8% (Equation (1)). Because the matched and exposed Cu {111} plane has the lowest growth velocity for octahedral Cu self-assemblies, we believed that CDs preferentially deposited on Cu {111} plane according to geometrical lattice matching theory (Fig. 3h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots-induced anisotropic evolution of self-assemblies
ce:section.ce:para:
Exploring mesoscale transformation of concave octahedral Cu mesocrystal, we found that self-assembly tended to the anisotropic evolution under the guidance of CDs (Fig. 2d–i). In this part, the anisotropic evolution of self-assemblies was controlled by changing relative dose of obtained CDs. The anisotropic evolution was evaluated by R, which representing the relative growth rate of crystal along <001> with <111> direction. The value of R was calculated using Equation (2): [38]
(2)
R =
V
〈
001
〉
V
〈
111
〉
where V <001> was the growth velocity along the direction of <001>, V <111> was the growth velocity along the direction of <001>. In fact, 2.5 μm octahedral (R = 1.73), 4.5 μm concave octahedral (R > 1.73) and 5.5 μm hexpods Cu mesocrystals (R ≫ 1.73) were obtained with the relative dose of CDs increasing from 0.5 (5), 1 (5) to 2 (5) (Fig. 4 a–c, Figs. S5a–S5c). The self-assemblies with larger size were obtained in relative larger dose of CDs, indicating that CDs induce crystallization of Cu. Finally, the self-assembly with the limited OS process exhibited a loose-stacking oriented structure at 4 (5) (Fig. S6). Octahedral, concave octahedral and hexpods Cu mesocrystal were obtained in increasing relative dose of CDs, corresponding to different states of persistent anisotropic evolution of Cu octahedral mesocrystal. This meant that the relative dose of CDs in reaction system would finally determine the stable shape of self-assembly through anisotropic growth. The transformation of crystal shape from octahedron to hexapods has been reported [39,40], and it concluded that crystals along <001> directions grew faster than other directions such as <111>. So we concluded that more CDs selectively deposited on the {111} crystal plane of self-assemblies via lattice matching, making the crystals along <111> directions grew lower and the value of R bigger. Therefore, more concave shapes were formed (Fig. 4a–c).

The self-assembled sphere and octahedron always coexisted in CDs matrix whether the coating polymer of CDs was TEPA, branched polyethyleneimine (bPEI) or citric acid (CA) (Fig. S6). In addition, the FETEM figures of bPEI-CDs and CA-CDs were shown in Fig. S7. Interestingly, octahedron was the main self-assembled shape when the coating polymer of CDs was TEPA, but it was sphere when the coating polymer of CDs was bPEI (Fig. S6a). Moreover, self-assembled spherical mesocrystal obtained in bPEI-CDs matrix would transform to octahedron and also exhibited anisotropic growth with the relative concentration ratio of CDs increasing (Figs. S6a–6b). These clues also verified that it was the special structure of “quantum dots” in CDs which changed the growth rate of {111} plane of Cu mesocrystals.

The increasing dose of CDs also leaded to anisotropic evolution of self-assembled Cu single crystal (Fig. 4d–f). No self-assembly was found at 0.5 (25) shown in Fig. S8a. Self-assembled octahedral single crystal with the R = 1.73, and spherical and truncated octahedral self-assemblies with 1 ≤ R < 1.73 were achieved separately at 1 (25) and 2 (25) (Fig. 4d–f). However, self-assembly could be hardly found at 4 (25) (Fig. S8d). Fig. S8c showed the semi-finished state of polyhedron which was similar to that of octahedral Cu single crystal: both semi-finished state had obvious self-assembled indication. We setup several control experiments to ensure how CDs and residual organics which coexisted in CDs solution affected the growth of Cu single crystals (controlled experiment 2). No size-defined self-assembly appeared when chitosan-acetic acid and TEPA solution was separately acted on the growth of Cu crystal (Fig. 4h–i). When just adding raw organics mixture of chitosan-acetic acid and TEPA as growth additive, obtained result was similar with at 4 (25) (Fig. S8d, Fig. 4g), suggesting that it was residual organics which functioned on the growth of Cu self-assembly at 4 (25). Therefore, there would be competitive adsorption between CDs and the residual organics on Cu {111} plane, and the adsorption of residual organics is favorable in high relative concentration of NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-induced anisotropic evolution mechanism of self-assemblies
ce:section.ce:para: It was known that the crystals with concave surface could be synthesized by two strategies: site-specific dissolution and directionally controllable overgrowth [41]. In this study, it was mentioned that carbon dots selectively deposited on {111} crystal plane of Cu self-assemblies via lattice match-induced epitaxial growth, making the crystals along <111> directions grow lowly, so the quantity of carbon dots on self-assemblies would determine the morphology and value of R of self-assemblies. We concluded that more carbon dots on self-assemblies led to bigger value of R while less carbon dots on self-assemblies led to smaller value of R.

Because less CDs chelated with Cu2+ in relative high concentration of NaOH, the precursors possibly became [Cu(OH)3(CDs)]− and [Cu(OH)2(CDs)2] at 1 (25) and 1(5). So when Cu self-assemblies were formed by reduction of precursors, various chelated CDs simultaneously deposited on their {111} plane via lattice matching-induced epitaxial growth, which leaded to the absorptive capacity of CDs on self-assemblies at 1 (25) lower than at 1 (5), and the value of R of self-assemblies decreased at the same time. This is why concave octahedron (R > 1.73) was obtained at 1 (5) while octahedron (R = 1.73) were obtained at 1 (25), and hexpods (R ≫ 1.73) was obtained at 2 (5) while sphere and truncated octahedron (1 ≤ R < 1.73) obtained at 2 (25) (Scheme 1 ). Interestingly, with the relative dose quantity of CDs increasing from 0.5 to 4, more CDs exited in solution, the value of R increased when keeping the volume ratio of OH− (10 M) and Cu2+ (0.1 M) to be 5, while the opposite result occurred when keeping the volume ratio of OH− (10 M) to Cu2+ (0.1 M) at 25. We attributed these results to the competitive adsorption between CDs and residual organics on self-assemblies: High relative concentration of OH− would benefit the adsorption of residual organics on self-assembly. So the adsorption of residual organics resulted in the decreased CDs deposited on Cu plane of {111}when keeping the volume ratio of OH− (10 M) to Cu2+ (0.1 M) at 25, as a result, the value of R of self-assemblies decreased, and the self-assembly disappeared at 4 (25).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembled hexpods Cu mesocrystals for SERS substrate
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-coated hexpods Cu mesocrystals were used as a surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrate to detect organic molecule and biological molecular for the following reasons: 1. they are easy to be combined with organic and biological molecules by electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bond and chemical bond because of the plentiful functional groups of CDs; 2. Their ordered self-assembled structure provides a large number of nano-sized active sites; 3. Good dispersibility and stability of Cu mesocrystal improve the reproducibility of Cu based SERS substrate.

After 40 days in air, only two faint diffraction peaks were found in the XRD patterns of hexpods Cu mesocrystals (Fig. 5 c). They also kept stable when the environmental temperature was less than 160 °C (Fig. 5d). So our obtained hexpods Cu mesocrystals exhibited very good thermodynamic stability. As a SERS substrate, the Raman signal of 4-ATP on it even arose when the concentration was 10−8 M (Fig. 5a). Six sites absorbed by 10−6 M 4-ATP were shown in Fig. 5b, indicating the good reproducibility of this hexpods Cu mesocrystals substrate. As shown in Fig. 5a, the Raman peak of 4-ATP at 1591 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 attributing to a
1 vibrational modes had a red shift of 10 cm−1, and significant enhancement can be observed as the result from “hot-spots” architecture of hexpods Cu mesocrystals causing by the EM effect [42]. But the maximum enhancement appeared at 1434, 1389 and 1138 cm−1 in our system, and moved to sites at 1428, 1380 and 1149 cm−1. They could be assigned to b
2 vibrational modes of 4-ATP [43]. The enhancement of b
2 modes was explained in term to charge transfer (CT) mechanism by Osawa [44], However, Huang et al. discovered that b
2 modes of 4-ATP only appeared at a high-power laser, and coupling reaction that transformation of 4-ATP to 4, 4’-dimercaptoazobenzene (DMAB) was strongly related to this condition [45], In a word, the enhanced peaks at 1428, 1380 and 1149 cm−1 should be assigned to a
g mode of DMAB rather than b
2 mode of 4-ATP. The Raman spectra of 10−6 M p-ATP on Cu self-assemblies with various structures are shown in Fig. S9, Raman spectra of p-ATP on octahedral, spherical, hexpod self-assemblies have very little difference, their maximum enhancement appeared at 1434, 1389 and 1138 cm−1. However, the maximum enhancement appeared at 1591 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 on concave octahedral Cu self-assemblies, which means strong EM effect derived from its concave structure [30].

In this study, we prove that carbon dots (CDs) are promising for constructing highly ordered structure. Both lattice matching and organic-inorganics interaction of CDs benefit from oriented crystallization and aggregation, so self-assembled Cu single crystal with large size and mesocrystal with plentiful “hot spot” are obtained in CDs matrix under the regulation of the nucleation rate of primary nanoparticles. The lattice matching of CDs with the Cu crystal plane of {111} lead to oriented evolution of self-assemblies, thus the morphologies of self-assemblies are easily controlled by adjusting the relative dose of obtained CDs. Further, hybrids CDs barely affect the SERS activity of Cu self-assemblies because they act as a protector and efficient interface for optical conversion and electron transfer. Using p-aminothiophenol (4-ABT) molecule as a probe molecule on this SERS substrate, the detection limit is 10−8 M.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41671302), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2014B090901037, 2015A010105022, 2016A010103026); Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City (201604020134, 201607010183).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.03.090.
